text: Hello and welcome to this episode of This is My Architecture. I'm Kapil and with me I have Hari
from Mobilewala. Hari, welcome to the show. Thank you for having me here Kapil. Hari, can you tell
us a little bit about Mobilewala? Sure. Mobilewala is a consumer data and artificial intelligence
company. We work with businesses, enterprises to understand, model and predict customer behavior
using data which helps those company to acquire, retain and grow their customer base.
Okay, and Hari, a few months ago you adopted Amazon Elasticsearch Service. Can you tell us
what was the use case that you were trying to solve? Sure. So, Mobilewala being a data-centric
company, so we have a lot of data exchange happening between us and our customers. And
our entire platform is resigning on AWS and we use S3 buckets as our primary mechanism to
share data with each of our clients. So, we wanted to build a proactive near real-time anomaly
detector system to look into the access logs and alert us if there is any suspicious activity
or to help us find if there is any compromise keys. So, you needed a system that would alert
you when access patterns to data changed and you needed this to be almost in near real-time. How did
you implement this using Amazon Elasticsearch? So, we used S3 access logs with the Amazon Elasticsearch
anomaly detector feature. The key is the anomaly detector feature which was available in the Amazon
Elasticsearch. So, each of the client data sits on their individual bucket. So, all the access triggers
an access logs which gets delivered into a dedicated access log bucket. So, the moment the log lands
in this bucket, it triggers a Lambda function. This Lambda function looks into the access logs,
passes through the values, extract the values and converts them into a fields which then inserts
into the Elasticsearch. So, this is where we have configured the anomaly detectors to look into these
access log records for each client and learn from the access pattern and which in turn helps us detect
if there is any change in access pattern or change in the source country. This, if it detects something
suspicious or if it finds an anomaly, it triggers an alert. We use AWS SNS to send us email notifications,
which our service team receives and they do an investigation to further check if there is any cause of concern
and take necessary actions. That's a really interesting implementation, Hari. As you build this system,
were there any challenges that you had to overcome? Yup, we did face some challenges. When the Elasticsearch
anomaly detector was initially introduced, at that time, the IP address is not supported. So, the detector
cannot work on IP address field. It requires a numeric field and what we have is IP address from the access logs.
So, at the Lambda, we were using a geolocation database to map each IP addresses to a country,
source country and for each country, we assigned a unique numeric ID and we also add additional custom
fields which we require later in our Elasticsearch anomaly detector configuration. So, that field is
the client name. All these fields are custom fields which the Lambda inserts into the Elasticsearch
for each of the access log records. In the Elasticsearch, when we are conferring in the detector,
so we use the client name to filter out each client's access log separately and we use the country ID,
which is converted from the IP address to a numeric value as the field for the detector to work on.
And Hari, you have had this system running for several months now. Can you tell us a bit about the
performance and the outcome so far? So far, the system has functioned flawlessly. We receive close
to 18 to 20 million access log records every month and the system is able to pass through all them. It
was able to learn from the access pattern. For each of the client, it was able to build a model and it
was able to detect any anomalies. That's fantastic, Hari. And have you received any such anomaly detections so far?
Yeah, we did receive a couple of them and after investigation, it turned out to be a change in the access time,
which the detector noticed from how it learned from the historical records. And that got, we got an alert and after we
investigated, we organized as a valid access. Got it. So that's a very interesting implementation.
And do you have any plans to extend this to other sources of data, perhaps?
Yeah, we wanted to. So right now, only access logs are in this particular system. We wanted to see if we
can bring our system metrics like CPU, memory and disk metrics also into Elasticsearch and use a
anomaly detector to help us detect any spike in the system activities or any unusual behavior, unusual
activity and so that we can take actions faster. That's fantastic. Hari, thanks a lot for sharing all these
insights with our viewers. And thank you for watching this episode of This is My Architecture.
